speaker: interviewer
text: Comma and then put C-A, you know, maybe, just for kicks. Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to [INAUDIBLE], sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [INAUDIBLE] where the interview will be, will be about-- about a third from the beginning-- well, 20%, you know, the early. and then the bulk of it during the war, your [? hiding, ?] the occupation, the ghetto life. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Nathan Skaletzky, spelled N-A-T-H-A-N. Skaletzky is S-K-A-L-E-T-Z-K-Y. Nathan was born as Noucham.

speaker: interviewee
text: Noucham.

speaker: interviewer
text: Skaletzky. N-O-U-C-H-A-M [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Korets, Poland. . That's K-O-R-E-T-S, Poland, on August 9--

speaker: interviewer
text: 1918. My name is Donna Kanter-- D-O-N-N-A K-A-N-T-E-R. And we're-- we're in Encino, California. That is the county of Los Angeles, in the United States of America, in California. Today's date is January 24, 1995. Would you please say your name and your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Nathan Skaletzky. I was born August the 9th, 1918, in town, Korets.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the state Wolin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today, Nathan?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Skaletzky, would you talk about your life in Korets as a young boy growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come from Korets. Our town was 600 years old. It was 600 years ago, a Polish nobleman, his-- the name is Korets. He invite Jews to come in over there. He came in, and he built a synagogue from his money in a [INAUDIBLE] that's a Ukrainian synagogue, and a Polish. And that-- from that time on, 600 years ago, we developed the town. It was a beautiful town. From all three sides was a river. For the other side was a big fortress. And we were developing. And we were there 5,000 Jews in-- in Korets. There had beautiful teenagers, girls and boys. We had 17 shuls. We had all kinds of organizations. And the-- the ground was very good ground. We had the best fruits, the best meats in world there. And I-- my father and my grandfather, came from our families, they were making wine-- mead. And they were making for the Poles. They're making that till 1937. In '37, Poles came in. They say they want from each liter, a dollar-- a zloty. We couldn't afford it. We closed it up. And we went broke. And this was very bad the last couple years. Till the Russian came in, they closed the big towns like Warsaw. And we start doing the same buying mead. We started doing a lot of business. I went to school. And it was a wonderful time the couple years when the Russians were over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the Russians came in 1939--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was your life like-- your Jewish life-- your-- with your family and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was religious. So I had to go with him. And I had to go to shul once on Saturdays. But I-- I was a Zionist. First I belonged to a Mizrahi. And then I changed my mind, and I went to a left organization, Gordonia. And I went there. We-- they gave us some kind of lectures and entertainment. On Saturday, we used to have lectures, and then we had music. And boys and girls together, was-- but we-- we could not dance-- like a [? slow ?] dance. But otherwise, all kind of dances together we could do it. It was a beautiful life and beautiful children. And till the Russian came in, then we had to change our system in-- for the Russia system. I myself know several languages. I know-- my first language was Jewish. Then in-- I had to learn Polish good. And then-- then I learned Ukrainian, because we were living a bit in Ukraine. Then the German came in. We had Germans. I have to learn German. And of course we had to learn Russian. So I know about six languages, but, of course, they helped me out a little bit to survive. I count on the languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me about your sister and brother? And tell me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were three children. I was the oldest one. Then we had a brother, [? Leo. ?] And I had a-- my sister, Hannah. [? Leo ?] was not that good pupil, so he went to [INAUDIBLE] in a-- in a store for Poland. The girl mind was a very good, and-- and she was studying-- very smart. And she was three years, four years younger than I am. And my brother was two years younger than I am. So I was the oldest one. And we lived together till the Germans invaded. The Germans came in, and-- and they start looking for Jews. One time on Tisha B'Av, they came in, and they was catching young Jewish boys. That time my brother, [? Leo ?] was working next door by a Gentile. So we thought he's safe there. But somebody was telling the police. They came in. They took him away. Took away another boy. In that time, that day, they took out 450 Jews. They took them away about seven miles from us. They put them in a ditch, and a big grenade to kill all of them. But we didn't know at that time. We only knew when they kill us in [INAUDIBLE]. At that time they [INAUDIBLE] returned, and they added 2,500 people. That time we knew already that they were not playing with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we-- before we begin with the arrival of the Germans, could we talk a little bit about what your life was with your family and your sister and brother? What did you do together, the kind of work your parents did, your Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. All right. Like I told you, up to '37 we were making a living. And my sister was going to school, already finished school. And I was helping my father a little bit. And my brother was working like a magazine. In '30-- '37, the Poles demanded from us from each liter a-- a zloty. We couldn't make it. We closed it up. And it was very bad. In our town, they didn't have enough food to eat. But we were ashamed. But we didn't have anything. So my mother was cooking plain water to show the neighbors that she is cooking. But we didn't have anything. And the last couple of years was very bad till the Russian came in. But I was a teenager. I was belonging to organization, Gordonia. I was going for all kind of lectures. I was enjoying myself. Of course I didn't have no clothing-- very bad. Came winter, I grow up. I didn't have a winter jacket. So-- but a summer jacket I'm running to them, and I'm staying there. And I could-- couldn't afford to have a winter jacket. It was 20 below zero in our time, I had to put up with that-- this kind of life in Korets, the last couple years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- what-- describe Korets, what it was like. Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Korets, like I told you, was a beautiful city. From all three sides was a river. And we were on a hill. The whole city was on a hill. And of course, there was a main street, but the name was [? Katchuska-- ?] the main street. And side streets-- in the main street it was bricks. The other one we have the soft [? stone, ?] [? mainly ?] was-- in the fall, it was a lot of rain, it was a lot of dirt. It was a very big thing to go through. Because was-- everything was wet. But this doesn't stop us. Yeah of course we was trying to make a living there. But I was young. And I didn't care. But I had good entertainment. And there was the nicest girl in the town-- very beautiful girls and beautiful boys. But they used to call us the seven engineers. We were going on the streets. I decided with working, we didn't have a-- where to go to work. 90% of us worked. In the hundreds, we were. We walk on the street, and at night. But we couldn't find a job. And we didn't have it. They call us engineer-- measuring the sidewalks. But we were beautiful people. Beautiful, nice, good-looking people, with good hearts. But you couldn't help it. It was a very bad time. Because our town was four miles from the border. So they took away half of the population there. So-- but didn't half on what to live. They only have 50%. The other population, the Christians was a-- they were farmers so it was hundreds stores with all kinds of foods. But on the other half, so nobody could make a living. Very, very, very bad for Poland it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Jewish population? Would you talk about that, the fact that there was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I told you, our town was a beautiful town. We had two rabbis. One was-- he was a-- his-- his grandfather was very famous. Rabbi [INAUDIBLE]. Very famous. Very religious Jews know about that. He was a philosopher. And he wrote books. We-- we used to-- we lived about a block away from them. And that rabbi, [INAUDIBLE] his name is. He took a whole block. He had a shul there. He had a big house. He had a big garden. Matter of fact, once a year, my-- my grandfather had to give him honey to make a cake. And give everybody-- I take a piece of cake that was [NON-ENGLISH]. So my grandfather had this from every year that he had to give the honey and make the cake. And give-- and the-- and the rabbi used to divide it-- each one. Everybody get a piece of cake in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a beautiful town. The ground was good. But we couldn't make a living. But maybe 5% make a living. The rest of them was-- we were very poor. But the air was beautiful. You do not consider that-- that time that you can go-- come in to us. A lot of people came into to us in the summertime, to spend the summer. Because it was a beautiful surround-- town. And they coming into enjoy the summer by us. We never-- the weather was beautiful. We never sweat. Always it was like-- now, that time it was 25, 30 degree in Celsius. It was beautiful. Winter time was very cold. If we want [INAUDIBLE], they was warm up with boots and it was-- winter was very bad. Cold. And the whole winter was snowing in the house. You're lucky you-- you wash yourself once a week there. Because it was very cold. But in the summer we lived up and we went to the river. We-- every Saturday we-- it was full of people by the river. Enjoy yourself swimming, jumping in. The whole town was a Jewish town. It was in the hundreds there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean it was a Jewish town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, about 90% was Jewish. The-- in our time, we are 5,000 Jews. So 90% were Jewish people living in the streets. And everybody to live there most they have their own houses. We had our-- our grandfather house. We lived in the grandfather's house. It was a nice house. It was brick. And this time, a drink or something, we never regret. Because we had a very good house built in 1914-- practically new. So we-- that-- from that point of view we had at least there to live-- in have beautiful house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course we-- everybody was looking forward to go to Israel-- maybe to-- to go there. But they only let go two, three people a year from us. So, who went there? The best ones. You know, from-- for so many, the smartest ones-- two three, four people a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was trying to go-- dream about. But it would take maybe 15 years I go. Because they were smarter than I am. More active. That's for in Israel. They are very, the smartest. There's called the top people, they are there. That's why they're so smart. Because they used to pick the smartest one, the best one. The first pioneer. And that's why they're so successful, because the best from-- from us went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was very religious, fanatic religious. He had to go three times a week-- three times a day in the synagogue. He didn't-- first the synagogue. He didn't care about food. Like I told-- one time, he did-- couldn't make a living. So he went to the village. They had iron pots, and they broke. So he learned how to put them together. He went to the village to get-- to help out the farmers to put together the iron pots. He didn't leave a-- a dollar in the house. We were three children on a Monday. My mother took me to the hospital, took some shots. Was 3 o'clock in the afternoon. We didn't have anything in the mouths. We passed by our second cousin, and she saw there our faces. And she gave us a piece of bread. 3 o'clock in the afternoon. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can I tell you about that? Except it was-- was very bad till the-- the Russia came in but from our town, from our-- But I was young. I was spending the time pretty good in the Zionist organization. But materially, it was very bad. Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I told you before that 50%, half the farmers, they took them to Russia. So our town was a few miles from-- from the border. They took away all the farmers there. So we only had 50% of farmers. On top of them, the Poles give us a big taxes. We are poor people. We are existing from hundreds of years of making wine. Here they're coming in. We are selling a liter of wine for a-- for a zloty. He comes in, he says he wants a zloty from each liter for himself. We had to close up. We could-- we didn't have no choice. My grandfather tried to sell it. Not to pay him, they put him in jail. So after a couple of weeks in jail, he didn't-- just stop selling it. But otherwise, the town was a beautiful town. You never see a town like that on a hill-- beautiful fresh air. And the food was natural food-- the best food in the world. The climate was good. And the people was good. Right now nobody there. Not even one human being there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then-- then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in '39, first they came in-- the Russian came into us. We didn't know. They start coming with airplanes. Somebody came in, they say that Russia came in. We came out, we greet them. After a couple of days, everybody goes to work, everybody goes to school. My father opened up a factory. Start making wine. In-- of course, they took away half, Poland. They used to-- they used to get wine from big cities, from Warsaw, from Lemberg. Now everything is closed up. And the Russians like-- like to drink. So my-- my father-- and then we made mead, they call it, made from honey. We get a lot of honey by us. We cannot sell them anymore. They-- the farmers cannot sell them anymore. So they bring over to us. My father start making mead and wine. Sell it to the Russians-- sell it to everybody. Making money a lot. Of course, it was fully dangerous. They say that you are [NON-ENGLISH]. You're-- they're gonna put you to Siberia. So be our partner. The partner came in. He says, I don't want to be. And he left. So my father stayed there, and he made the wine. And he made money. It was pretty good. I went to school. I was-- was a lot of food. And I bought clothing. It was very good for a couple of years. I was going to school. And I was very physically-- I went to all kind of games. The year the Russians [? stated ?] that you have to be strong to be a military. You have to go to courses. And then they give you a medal. The call it [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. The medal was that you have to go 25 miles for some many time. You have to run four miles so much in 10 minutes. You have to swim. And the reason what I finished that. Was a lot of Jewish guy. We came in, and they try us out. We have to throw grenades. We have to throw grenades in a window. All the Jewish guys are never ever used to that. Nobody could do it. Me, living not far from the river, all the time throwing stones, I threw the stone right in-- in the window-- like a grenade. And they tell me give me a course, you're gonna make it. Of course-- and I made it. I had to go swimming. I had to four miles, and more miles. And I-- and I made all the courses. That day would start the war with the Germans. It was a day they called, [NON-ENGLISH] day. And that time I had to get a medal. And the war start. There was a teacher. He was a nationalist Tatyewsky. He was a bad guy. He-- he was a teacher by us. And then he start teaching-- teaching for Russians. He was teaching religious songs and for Russia, he could teach something a bit. So they went into the [INAUDIBLE] over there and they would talk about him. And they let him go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was-- he was looking for the cousin of my wife. He was looking-- the Germans came in. He was looking for them to kill-- to catch him, but he went away to Russia. But me, because I was with-- he was in the-- in the military, he was training, and I was doing all kind of good stuff there, he-- he liked me. He-- as a matter of fact, he told me, why didn't you run away? Why you have to stay here? I says, I-- I was not a Communist. I didn't mix up. Why should I run away? But he knew better than I did. That it doesn't matter if you're a Communist or not. You are Jewish. You get killed. That Tatyewsky, he was a son of a pope-- of-- what do you call this? Ukrainian father-- he was very educated. So for the Germans-- he was a-- worked with the Germans. And I was working a stone mine. And he was the-- the secretary. And he was paying me a few pennies, that guy. As a matter of fact, [INAUDIBLE] run away together with the Germans to Germany-- there-- I was later told. What happened after you went for the medal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get the medal. Because that day, that Sunday, started the war. So didn't make no entertainment. They didn't have a parade-- do anything. But what they did to me, I was prepared for that. I was getting strong muscles. I was getting very strong, and that's what saved my life. Because I was strong. I could run. And during that time, with the bad times, I was-- I could jump. I could-- I did everything. Because that couple years we had very good in the house. I-- I'm-- I get good food. And I grow up. So much I was very strong. And this-- because of that, it saved me. Because my-- but I was prepared for that. Like-- like-- this was preparing me for bad time. That-- that-- that day, Tisha B'Av, when they took my brother there, later on they want-- they came into us. I run out to the back door. And I came into a-- a big stones which we had to jump. I jump from the stones down to the river. From both sides was policeman. And I jumped from the stones down, down, to the-- and-- and the other farmers they put a-- a crest on him, to see how I'm doing. And I jumped up. I didn't hurt myself. I run away from them. I-- I run away. I didn't-- they didn't catch me in that day. In that day they killed 450 people. I run away that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us when that was, the dates?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not exactly-- I know in Jewish they call it Tisha B'Av. That was the day the [INAUDIBLE]-- the Germans used to pick up a day like that. On Tisha B'Av, came in a guy says they are catching young Jewish people, watch yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- my brother was working next door by-- by Christian. So he was safe. And each our houses was a-- was a Jewish star. But him, he didn't have. So police didn't go to him. I know by us there was a Jewish star. They had to go. So I'm look in the window, I see a police comes in. They come in the house. I'm running to the garden. Then they going away. I'm coming back. This time, they went into the garden looking. So I run to the river, and I jumped to-- and that was make me my muscles. And they saved me that time. And that time my brother was caught and killed. Would you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, the only young-- young people. Because my grandfather was 72 years old. He was laying in the-- laying. They didn't touch him. They came in, policeman they didn't touch him. They were looking for young ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time they caught in our city [INAUDIBLE] 450 people. They took him seven miles from there. We didn't know. We thought they took them out for work. And seven miles from us, and-- and they threw grenade to them and killed them-- 450 people. And my-- and my brother was there on that day. This was already the second time.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time, they made a list from the smartest one, 120 people. All the big business men they took them. They says they-- they want them to go to work with him. So they-- they came in free to them. They took them-- took them away. They kept them at a place-- hiding. And they forced them to write letters that they are working in a-- in a camp. And they're asking for food. And they ask for clothing. And if they don't write in Jewish, then they tell the other guy should read. If he-- if he makes-- if he lies something, he gets beat up. So they have to write exactly. And they had made five, six letters. It took them two, three days to get the letters. And then the 120 smartest people they killed. And then every couple of months, they came in with a letter saying that their people working over there. They need clothing. Many are women with clothing, and with-- also begging to bring their clothing and food. They're going 40 miles. The police, Ukrainian police waiting for them, beat them up, took the clothing away, threw everything away. And this was two times like that. That was 120. Then at-- at that time it was 150. And the third time was Tisha B'Av, 450. And then came in-- I was working at that time in a sugar factory. Me and my father beating up stones-- calcium-- for the sugar. And when-- if they catch Jewish people not working, they don't care if they work or not. But if they-- they not standing right now by work, they take them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was working in that-- I didn't go home-- in the sugar factory beating up stones. And my-- my day work, then at night I'm back in the other workers. We take his place and beat up the stones. Beat up the stones in order to stay there not to go back to the city. There was three nights and three days people working on that thing. Then on the third night, with-- with us together, was the rabbi's son. Hold the thought for a moment. We'll-- we'll pick up in a minute. We have to--
